items.q: What is the difference between lateral, medial and terminal moraine?
items.correct: Lateral moraine forms along the sides of a glacier from rockfall; medial moraine forms where two lateral moraines merge; terminal moraine marks the maximum extent at the snout.
items.misconception: Moraines are all the same thing — just piles of rocks.
items.random_wrong: Lateral moraine is the youngest and terminal moraine is the oldest.
items.why: Their position records glacial history. Students lump them as 'rocks'. Distinguishing them is essential — terminal moraines mark past climate maxima; medial moraines reveal tributary glaciers.

items.q: What is the difference between glacial abrasion and plucking?
items.correct: Abrasion: rock embedded in the glacier base scrapes the bedrock smooth, creating striations. Plucking: meltwater refreezes onto bedrock, then movement rips chunks of rock away.
items.misconception: Abrasion and plucking are different words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Abrasion is when wind erodes the glacier and plucking is when rain does.
items.why: Both processes act together but produce different evidence: abrasion leaves smooth scratched surfaces; plucking leaves jagged surfaces. Students conflate them — recognising striations vs roches moutonnees is exam-critical.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.
It's Relph. “Tuan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Ruth Glass. “Doreen Massey” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Liverpool European Capital of Culture?
items.why: Exact answer: 2008 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
